parameters: Select one of the options to learn more about the game.

This game has a very important time system. Specifically, there are 7 days (Sunday-Saturday) and 4 time periods per day: Morning, Afternoon, Night, & Midnight. Different quests occur at different day/period combinations.
You can always see the current day and time in the menu, on the bottom left.
To skip ahead to a certain day, you can use the clock in Tsunade's Kitchen. This will skip you to the morning of the selected day.

There are also various ways to skip time periods. For one example, you can "study" in your room, which will skip to the next time period (in this case, night would become midnight). Most quests will naturally skip your time period forward as you complete them, too.
Lastly, sleeping in your bed will always skip you to the next day's morning.
Most quests are locked at the start of the game, but become unlocked with progress. The \c[17]Ninja Path\c[0], accessed via the menu, serves as the main interface for viewing quests.
There are three main sections of the Ninja Path. The first is \c[17]Available\c[0], which shows unlocked quests and their details.

Generally, each quest listing will include a time for the quest, a location, a description, and objective(s). It's a good habit to read a new quest's description, which often shows requirements for beginning the quest and/or fully completing it.
You can also click on a quest to "select" it, which will turn its text yellow in the list and place it in the \c[17]Quest Tracker\c[0].
The \c[17]Quest Tracker\c[0] and its currently selected quest will become visible in the top right area of the game view. This is a visual aid to help you stay focused and does not impact gameplay.
The \c[17]Quest Tracker\c[0] is enabled by default but can be disabled in 'Options > General > Enable Quest Tracker'.

There are two ways to know if you have completed a quest. The first is that it may move to the \c[17]Completed\c[0] section of the Ninja Path. This generally means that the quest is done for good and will not be continued in future versions.

OR, a completed quest may remain in the \c[17]Available\c[0] section, but it's final objective will be revealed as '\c[17]To be continued\c[0]'. This generally means that the quest will be continued in a future game version.
Lastly, the third section of the Ninja Path is \c[17]NEW CONTENT\c[0], where you can find details of what is new in the current version of the game.
The \c[17]Status\c[0] submenu holds key information about your current stats and game progress.
Its first section is \c[17]Quest Stats\c[0], which displays current stats related to the story, as well as how to improve them.

The second section is \c[17]Affection Stats\c[0], which displays current affection levels with your romantic interests. Improving these generally involves completing quests that involve the girls, respectively.

The third section is \c[17]Game Progress\c[0], which displays the number of quests that have been unlocked out of all included in the current game version. Note that this only shows how many quests are unlocked, not completed. To learn more about discerning a quest's completion, you should select 'Quest System' from the Tutorial.
The fourth section is \c[17]General\c[0], which displays basic information about your experience (EXP).

The fifth section is \c[17]Battle Stats\c[0], which displays detailed information about your battle stats. These are increased via both leveling up and assigning ability points (A.Points) to your character. To learn more, select 'Stats & Ability Points' from the Tutorial.
\c[17]Ability Points\c[0] are awarded to you upon leveling up. Keep an eye on your \c[3]A.Points\c[0] amount - whenever you have more than 0, it means you can spend them to boost your stats.
In short, battle stats are important for ensuring your character is strong and can win battles. Jutsus also scale with any one or combination of these stats (usually attack or chakra attack).

Inside the submenu, you can select 'Allocate' to parse through each stat of the character and spend your ability points to increase them individually (you can either click on each stat or use your keyboard's left/right arrow keys). Note that the 'Cost/Available' indicates the cost of ability points for leveling up the selected stat as well as the remaining ability points you have.

Generally, leveling up a specific stat will eventually increase the cost of ability points to continue doing so. In this example, after spending enough ability points on HP, it now costs 2 ability points to increase HP.
Once you allocate your ability points, you can select 'Finish' or simply exit the menu. To refund all your points and start from zero, you can select 'Revert'.
Reverting will simply refund all your allocated ability points back to you, you technically won't lose anything by doing it - just remember to re-allocate your refunded points!
\c[17]Equipment\c[0] can be found in this game by defeating enemies, finding it in the world, completing certain quests, and more. It is a good way to boost your character's battle stats.
There are five types of equipment: weapons, head armor, body armor, boots, and accessories.
The easiest way to equip your armor is to simply click 'Optimize', which will select the most mathematically efficient combination from your unlocked and wearable equipment.

You can also manually equip armor from your collection by selecting any of the five options in the Equipment menu. Note that some equipment has a level requirement to wear it, which will be indicated in its description.
Do you want to go to sleep?
You sleep through the night without any problems.
It's time to start the day.\
